html:
Photon Energy N.V. (P7V.F): distressed solar equity where the bad news is real, but the wipeout price may be too blunt

Photon Energy is no longer the clean “survived the solar downturn” story the older drafts assumed. The market is punishing something specific and legitimate: a liquidity squeeze around the Green EUR Bond 2021/2027, a postponed February 2026 coupon, the bankruptcy petition of the Polish trading subsidiary, and a delayed 2025 annual report. That is why the stock is near €0.31, down roughly 65% from its 52-week high, with an equity value of only about €19 million.

The investable question is not whether Photon is pristine. It is whether the entire parent equity should be priced like a near-zero stub when the operating solar asset base is still producing, the worst Polish DSR issue appears ring-fenced, and several noncore cash drains are being cut away.

Photon owns and operates a 134.7 MWp proprietary PV portfolio across the Czech Republic, Slovakia, Hungary, Romania and a residual Australian asset. In the preliminary Q4 2025 report, FY2025 revenue was €90.1 million, EBITDA was €5.1 million, operating cash flow was €20.6 million, and equity was €52.4 million. Those headline earnings were bad, but they mix together three very different things: a valuable IPP portfolio, a set of subscale/loss-making service activities, and a distressed Polish capacity-market/trading subsidiary.

The variant perception is that the market is treating all three as one insolvency blob. They are not equal. The Investments segment, which houses the owned power plants, generated €21.0 million of external revenue and €18.2 million of external EBITDA in 2025. That one segment alone produced more EBITDA than the entire current market capitalization. The consolidated number collapsed because corporate overhead, engineering losses, the Polish capacity-market dispute, and one-off impairments consumed the asset-level cash flow.

The recent news is ugly, but it also narrows the problem. In February 2026, Photon delayed a €1.282 million quarterly coupon on its €80 million Green Bond due November 2027 after PSE started offsetting disputed Polish capacity-market receivables. In March, the Warsaw court rejected the injunction request, and Photon Energy Trading PL filed for bankruptcy. That sounds catastrophic until the details are read carefully. Photon disclosed that the Polish subsidiary contributed about €15.9 million of 2025 revenue but an EBITDA loss of €1.6 million after the €3.2 million provision. Before that provision it was profit-making, but it was not the owned solar portfolio. Management explicitly said the bankruptcy petition did not affect Polish PV O&M or electricity trading in Hungary and the Czech Republic.

This is the long setup: the stock has reacted as though the parent is finished, while the evidence points to a forced simplification. The Polish DSR/trading hit removes revenue, but it also forces management to stop defending a liquidity-consuming activity. Australia is being restructured too, with Photon exiting C&I EPC and O&M activities that generated about €6 million of 2025 revenue but negative EBITDA of €1.5 million. The company is being pushed toward the business the market should have wanted all along: own cash-generating solar assets, manage third-party renewable assets, sell technology where margins exist, and monetize development rights.

The April 2026 monthly report matters because it arrived after the panic. Power generation reached 16.9 GWh, up 24.3% year over year. Estimated electricity-sale revenue was €2.875 million, up 16.7% year over year, despite lower average realized prices. The portfolio average realized price was €174/MWh, with Czech assets at €687/MWh, Slovakia at €265/MWh, Hungary at €116/MWh, and Romania at only €59/MWh due to merchant “duck curve” pressure. That is the right way to underwrite this name: not off stale trailing losses, but off current-cycle generation and realized power economics by geography. The Czech, Slovak, and Hungarian assets still provide tariff-backed or partially protected cash flow; Romania is the volatile merchant option.

There is also a capital-light transition that the market is ignoring because the bond issue dominates the tape. Photon has been selected to provide asset-management services for Greenvolt’s 100 MW BUJ I & II battery storage plants in Hungary under an initial five-year agreement. Photon Water also signed its first commercial PFAS firefighting-foam decontamination contract with a UK fire-extinguisher manufacturer. These are not worth underwriting aggressively today, but they are important because they point to a less balance-sheet-heavy Photon: asset management, BESS services, PFAS remediation, O&M, and technology trading layered on top of the solar portfolio.

At €0.31, the stock trades at roughly 0.35x preliminary book value of €0.87 per share and around 0.9x FY2025 operating cash flow. That cash flow includes working-capital help, and the debt load is severe, so this is not a low-risk “cheap on multiples” stock. But the upside torque comes from the fact that almost any credible bond cure, standstill, asset sale, or clean audit turns the equity from a distressed option back into an asset-backed microcap.

The key assets are not theoretical. The balance sheet had €166.5 million of PPE, €10.6 million of liquid assets, and €52.4 million of equity at year-end 2025. The owned generation business has already absorbed weak weather, Romanian licensing delays, lower merchant prices, and still produced high asset-level EBITDA. Development value is also marked close to zero in the market price: the company sold the 20.4 MWp Domanowo project at ready-to-build status in December 2025 and still reported a 569.5 MWp development pipeline. In a normal market, investors would pay something for that pipeline. Today they are paying nothing.

Retail context is favorable in a contrarian way. This is not a crowded meme trade. It is a thin, ignored Frankfurt/Warsaw microcap with tiny volume, heavy insider ownership, and almost no institutional sponsorship. That makes the stock dangerous to enter or exit, but it also means the price can be set by forced sellers and bond panic rather than by a balanced view of asset value.

The bear case is simple: the delayed coupon becomes a wider bondholder conflict, the 2025 audit produces larger impairments, the equity covenant becomes a real problem, and the company is forced into a punitive restructuring or emergency equity raise. That is why this deserves a distressed-equity position size, not a normal small-cap allocation.

The base case is more interesting. Photon cures or restructures the missed coupon without wiping out shareholders, exits or contains the loss-making Polish/Australian pieces, publishes Q1 on May 29 and the 2025 annual report on July 31 without a parent-level death spiral, and the owned portfolio keeps producing monthly cash. In that case, the stock does not need a heroic multiple. A move to 0.7x stressed book value, or roughly a €39 million equity value, gets to about €0.65 per share.

The bull case is that the current crisis becomes the forcing event that rationalizes the group. If management sells noncore development rights, cuts corporate overhead, completes Romanian licensing, normalizes EBITDA toward €12 to €15 million, and either refinances or tenders the 2027 bond at a discount, the equity can re-rate toward book value plus option value for BESS/PFAS. That supports €1.10 to €1.25 per share without assuming a takeover.

Current price: about €0.31.

Bear target: €0.05, down 84%, if bondholders or emergency dilution effectively take the company.

Base target: €0.65, up 110%, if the coupon/audit crisis is contained and the market values Photon as a going-concern solar asset owner again.

Bull target: €1.20, up 287%, if restructuring turns into a real simplification story and normalized EBITDA lands in the low-to-mid teens.
